claim: The GBR has experienced six mass bleaching events since 1998, with 2016, 2017, 2020, 2022, and 2024 events driven by marine heatwaves of increasing frequency, reducing recovery windows between events.

source.title: Great Barrier Reef Marine Park Authority Reef Health updates

method: Reviewed GBRMPA Reef Health reports for bleaching event timeline, severity classifications, and aerial survey results.
limitations: Event summaries reflect available survey coverage, not all reefs.

claim: NOAA Coral Reef Watch degree-heating-week alerts at 5km resolution correctly identify bleaching-level thermal stress but satellite SST can differ from in-situ reef temperature by 1-3°C, requiring site-specific calibration.
method: Reviewed CRW methodology, DHW threshold definitions, and satellite SST validation literature.
limitations: 5km resolution averages across reef and open ocean pixels.
Cloud cover can cause data gaps during bleaching events.

claim: A 2019 Coral Reefs study documented the 2014-2017 global-scale coral bleaching event as the longest and most widespread bleaching event on record, affecting all three major ocean basins and causing significant mortality on reefs that had previously escaped severe bleaching.

source.title: The 2014–2017 global-scale coral bleaching event: insights and impacts

method: Reviewed the peer-reviewed Coral Reefs article documenting the 2014-2017 global bleaching event, including its geographic extent, duration, and impact on previously unaffected reefs.
limitations: The study covers the 2014-2017 event and does not reflect more recent bleaching events.
The global assessment aggregates data from multiple monitoring programs with varying methods and coverage.
Regional severity estimates depend on reporting infrastructure that is uneven across reef systems.
